title: Eschatology: Overview of Millennial Systems — Part 2, Postmillennialism and Premillennialism
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Lesson 5 on Eschatology_ Overview of Millennial Systems - Part 2 - Postmill and Premill.txt

outline.title: Review of Amillennialism
subpoints: No future physical reign of Christ on earth; the millennium is a present spiritual reign in heaven
Christ rules currently through the church; at His return, one general resurrection of just and unjust together
Associated with pessimism about history — world worsening, small remnant at the end
All three Reformed positions agree that tribulation is future or has already occurred; amillennial and postmillennial positions place it in the past (Nero's persecution, fall of Jerusalem)

outline.title: Postmillennialism — Core Teachings
subpoints: Classic postmillennialism holds to a future physical millennium of approximately a thousand years — this is commonly misunderstood
Not yet in the millennium; it is still future — a glorious kingdom fulfilling many Old Testament promises
The Olivet Discourse persecutions (Matthew 24-25, Mark 13, Luke 17) are mostly past — fulfilled in AD 66-70 destruction of Jerusalem
Universal Christianization: not that every person ever is saved, but at the peak of the kingdom era Christianity is universally dominant
The kingdom comes through the church's faithful gospel preaching empowered by the Spirit — no separate Jewish kingdom
Almost universally postmillennialists expect a great revival and ingathering of ethnic Israel into the church
Christ remains in heaven during the millennium, ruling through the Spirit; after the millennium He returns for a general resurrection

outline.title: Postmillennialism — Theonomy and Reconstructionism
subpoints: A segment of postmillennialists are called reconstructionists or theonomists
Classic postmillennialism expected God to bring the new order through gospel proclamation and Spirit-empowered conversion
Reconstructionists expect Christians to take an active, aggressive role in restructuring societal institutions
Theonomy (as a specific movement) advocates applying Old Testament civil laws to modern society
All Christians are theonomists in the sense that we follow the law of God; the specific movement wants OT civil law as the basis for national law

Even classic reconstructionists hold to a future physical millennium — the 'no-millennium' version is very recent (late 20th / early 21st century), associated with Federal Vision (Doug Wilson, Peter Leithart)
Strongest arguments: parables of the mustard seed and leaven — small beginnings grow to dominance; Romans 11 ingathering of Israel

outline.title: Historic Premillennialism — Core Teachings
subpoints: A future, glorious, literal thousand-year reign of Christ on earth follows His physical return — different from the current spiritual reign
The kingdom will be both political and spiritual — the King rules, including over unconverted people present during the millennium
A future period of severe persecution (Great Tribulation) precedes the return of Christ, through which the visible church passes
No pre-tribulation rapture — the 'rapture' teaching is found only in the Left Behind series, not the Bible
All three Reformed positions — amillennial, postmillennial, and historic premillennial — agree on a post-tribulational return of Christ
Two future resurrections: the righteous at Christ's return, the unrighteous at the close of the millennial reign (Revelation 20)
Name: premillennial because Christ's return is before (pre-) the millennium

outline.title: Historic Premillennialism vs. Dispensational Premillennialism
subpoints: Dispensational premillennialism is what most American evangelicals know as 'premillennialism'; it is a different system entirely
Dispensationalism would not be permitted at ordination in the Bible Presbyterian Church
Historic premillennialism is grounded in covenant theology — the church began with Abraham and is tied to the Abrahamic covenant
Dispensationalism separates Israel and the church as two entirely distinct peoples with separate purposes
The identity of the church and its relationship to Israel will be the subject of the next several lessons
Tribulation timing follows naturally from the identity of the church: if the church is spiritual Israel, it goes through tribulation meant for God's people

summary:
This fifth lesson continues the survey of millennial systems, covering postmillennialism and introducing historic premillennialism. After a brief review of amillennialism, Pastor Tito Lyro turns to postmillennialism, carefully correcting a widespread misconception: classical postmillennialism — as held by the Puritans, Jonathan Edwards, Charles Hodge, and B.B. Warfield — affirms a future literal millennium on earth. The idea that postmillennialism does not involve a future physical kingdom is a very recent development associated with the Federal Vision movement. The classic postmillennial view expects the gospel to spread through the church's ministry until Christianity is universally dominant, followed by a great revival among ethnic Israel; then Christ returns after the millennium for a general resurrection.

The lesson addresses the reconstructionist/theonomist wing of postmillennialism — thinkers like Rushdoony, Gary North, Greg Bahnsen, and David Chilton — who call not only for gospel transformation but also for the reimplementation of Old Testament civil laws. Pastor Lyro clarifies that while all Christians are 'theonomists' in the broad sense of following God's law, the specific movement goes further by seeking to apply OT civil law to national governance. The reconstructionist movement has had a historically divisive effect in churches.

The pastor then introduces historic premillennialism, the position of the Bible Presbyterian Church and his own. It holds that Christ will physically return before a literal thousand-year earthly reign. All three Reformed positions agree on a post-tribulational return of Christ; they disagree about whether that tribulation is past or future. Historic premillennialism expects the church to go through the future Great Tribulation — there is no rapture removal before it. The lesson closes by distinguishing historic premillennialism sharply from dispensational premillennialism, noting that dispensationalism would disqualify a candidate from ordination in the BPC. The key difference is the identity of the church in relation to Israel — the subject of the next several lessons.
transcript:
## Introduction

Well, we'll continue on our study of eschatology. We're going to be going on for a few more weeks as I keep on considering it. There's more and more things that I wanted to talk about, but we hopefully will finish before the Lord returns at some point. That's at least my plan because we're going to be done no matter what at that point. So, let's pray together. Father, I have been thinking that you're good to us. We thank you for your word. And we think that we can come together during Sunday's school and study it. We pray that you would be with the thoughts of our hearts this morning. We pray to bless not only me as I teach the adults, but all the other teachers as they teach the children. We pray that today your spirit would work lightly in us through your word for asking Jesus name. Amen.

## Review of Amillennialism

We defined Amalena as the last week. We saw that in that view, there is no physical future thousand year reign of Christ on earth. That reign is happening right now, but is a spiritual reign in heaven. That's what the millennium is happening now. It teaches that we're currently living in Christ's mind of Kingdom. And when it returns, there will be one general resurrection of that point. And both the Justin, the unjust will be raised at that point. So, that's kind of the summary of the Amalena position. And then there is also consistent with Amalenaism, this idea of the apostasy of the church and also the situation of the world getting worse and worse. Not a complete apostasy of the church, but the idea of when Jesus was born. The idea of when Jesus, if the days weren't cut short, even the elect will be deceived. And there will be just a small remnant left at the end sort of idea. So, in my mind, the Amalena position is one that is, if you believe it is because you're really convinced from the Bible, not because it sounds good, you know, if you feel good at that point.

## Postmillennialism — Core Teachings

Any question about what we talk about last week on Amalenaism? Before we continue today. All right, so today we'll attempt to define the other two views that have been considered by the formed church as Orthodox positions. What I mean by that is that if you're whole to Amalena, the Amalena views, you're whole to the post-Malena view of the pre-Malena view that I've made described today or today and next week, you are within the boundaries of reformed orthodoxy to put it more simply. You'd be able to be ordained in the Bible Presbyterian Church. If you outside of those things, then you would not be able to be ordained in our Presbyterian.

And remember that I'm only defining them, not proving them just yet. I'm only going to try to prove the one that I've standarded, and the one that I think is the one that the Bible teaches. And so I'm not trying to prove them at this point, and that there will be no debate about it during Sunday's school, though I'm more than happy to try to answer whatever questions you may have.

All right, so start with the idea — one thing that most people don't understand today is that in the post-Malena view, at least how it has classically been defined, and how it was the belief of everybody that believed in it at least up to about 50 years ago, the post-Malennial holds to a future physical millennium. The post-Malena view holds to a future physical millennium. That it's about a thousand years. There will be a glorious thousand year kingdom on earth for filling many prophecies of the Old Testament. That's something that's been missed today when people think of post-Malenaism.

The idea that post-Malenaism doesn't include that is a new kid in the block. Historically, that's not been the post-Malennial position. We're not in it yet. It's some future time future to us. That's how the Puritans saw the post-Malena, that's how both English and American, not all of them, but those who believe in post-Malenaism held to this view. That's what Jonathan Edwards held. This view, this is what the President and scholars like Hodge and the Hodges were filled and so on held. They saw some variation of how long the kingdom would last, but the usual agreement on the existence of it in the future.

They also teach that the period of severe persecution described in the synoptic gospel. So if you look at Matthew, Mark and Luke, all three of them describe this period of tremendous persecution that Christians will suffer, future to the writing of the three gospels. So Matthew and Mark and Luke saw that and future to them. And most people think that those descriptions equate to the Puritans Revelation 6 to 19, a period of great tribulation. And post-Malenaial position teaches that that already happened. And that already happened when Vespatian and then Titus took over an invaded Jerusalem in AD66 through 70. So the fall of Jerusalem is that great tribulation described in what's called the Olivet discourse. Matthew 24 and 25, Mark 13, Luke 17, and then Revelation 6 through 19.

By the way, I forgot to say that last week, that's also true of amillennialism. Almost all amillennialists believe that that period of tribulation that the church will face has already happened in the past. Those things have been all fulfilled already. There is one well-known amillennialist that doesn't hold to that — he wrote the commentary on the whole New Testament, his William Hendrixon. He still sees that as future, but that's not the standard amillennial or post-Malenaial position.

They also have an great spiritual emphasis in the kingdom of God, at least this millennium. The post-Malenaial see this kingdom as being universal. It would be marked by universal salvation and righteousness. And I mean one thing by universal, I don't mean that everyone over all ages will be saved, but at that moment in time Christianity will be universal on the world. That's what I mean by universal there. They see many of the Old Testament physical prophecies applied to this future age in a spiritual sense. And there will be no Jewish recognition in this kingdom. Though they do almost every single post-Malenaial that has written a book that I'm aware of do believe that there will be a great revival in Israel and the Jewish people will come to faith in Jesus Christ. And it will be part of the church as well. Not as a separate Jewish kingdom, but part of the church of Jesus Christ at that point.

And they say that the kingdom is going to come about — the means of inauguration, how this kingdom is going to come about, is that it will be established by the power of God working through the church and the preaching of the gospel. That that's what's going to commence the kingdom. The church is going to be so faithful. The spirit is going to use the gospel proclamation so much that the whole world is going to get saved. And that's how the millennium kingdom begins. And especially in this position is the conversion of the Jewish people to Christ, based on Romans 9, 10, 11, their view.

And the church is not to expect the coming of Christ before the world is converted. So first of all, the world is converted, then Christ returns to the earth. Now, the amillennial and premillennial positions teach that Christ won't return till the whole world is evangelized, but the post-mill says the whole world will not only be evangelized but also converted to faith in Christ. And therefore Christ returns after the millennium reign, the end of the millennium reign. And during the millennium, Christ will still be in heaven, ruling through his Spirit. And at the end of the thousand years, he will come to the earth, raise the dead, in a general resurrection. And that's why it's called post-million because it's after the millennium.

## Theonomy and Reconstructionism

Andrew has been asking about theonomy in the context of eschatology. Theonomy is called reconstructionism. And that is a segment of post-malennial belief, a segment of post-malennial people who are called also reconstructions. Now, classic post-malinism expected God to bring in that new order through the faithful service of the church and Christians with the emphasis on God's regenerating more and more people and bringing them into the church. Reconstructionists expect Christians to take a more active and aggressive role in changes of societal structures. The church and the Christians should be about also implementing the Old Testament civil government laws, that's part of the idea of theonomy.

We all are theonomists, meaning we believe that we should follow the law of God, right? The law of God is good. The law of God is perfect, and we follow it. But theologically, the term theonomy means it is applied to those that want to revive the Old Testament civil laws and apply to society today. So whatever was a crime in the Old Testament should become a crime today and so on. And that's part of the reconstructionist movement in post-malinism.

But even the reconstructionist would also be looking for a future thousand-year reign of Christ. It's still would be looking for a future, a thousand-year reign of Christ. It was in the 20th century that this view became more popular. The newest kid on the block is these people who think that there's no at all any future physical reign. That's the newest position. Late 20th century, early 21st century, is when actually gained some acceptance in the church. And that's mostly associated with the Federal Vision movement, men like Doug Wilson and Peter Leithart and Richard Gaffin and so on, they hold more to that position.

All right, so one of the strongest arguments for post-malinism is the parables of the kingdom in the Bible. What I mean by the parables of the kingdom? Both of them, Matthew and Luke, you find several parables that Jesus says the kingdom of God or the kingdom of heaven is like. Can you think of any? The mustard seed, right? And so a mustard seed, might be as small as an emergency. The mustard seed starts small and then dominates the tree, and then you have leaven, the kingdom of God is like leaven that starts as a small portion then but that takes over everything. So this idea of Christianity gradually taken over everything, they interpret that as indication of the truth of post-malinism.

Though other than the parables, I find that the argument is often a negative one, as in, no, amillennialism is wrong, and premillennialism is wrong, and so on, instead of spending time on positive biblical argument. The positive argument for it is not that present in at least more recent writing.

One good thing about post-malinism — they are a very optimistic position, right? Amillennial and dispensational premillennialism are pessimistic about the future of the church and Christianity in this age. Only post-malinism is optimistic. They are optimistic as it expects Christ to conquer all his enemies through the church's ministry, the gospel in this age, and post-malinists are optimistic about the future conversion of Israel as well.

The problem, a scary fact about this view is how many people who are mainstays in the faith hold to this view. You know, the Puritans, Jonathan Edwards held to this view, Charles Hodge, A.A. Hodge, B.B. Warfield, the Southern Presbyterian Church as a whole held to this view. Some modern reconstructionists, some of that are still around today — people like Rushdoonie, Gary North, Greg Bahnsen, David Chilton, Gary DeMar, Ken Gentry, these are all post-malennial people.

## Historic Premillennialism — Core Teachings

We're going to try to start then defining the primalennial view here. And as I do that, any of those of you who are up in a non-Reformed church, the primalennialism that you came in contact with is likely the one that's called dispensational primalennialism. That is not what we believe here, and I'll try to make that difference later on in the series. And most of the arguments that the amillennial opposition, the post-millennialists use against why you shouldn't be a premillennialist have to do mostly with that variety of dispensational premillennialism.

A dispensational premillennialist would not be ordained in our Presbyterian. That's different from what the Bible teaches. Even though that's probably the one you had the most contact with if you grew up in a non-Presbyterian church. If you grew up in the Presbyterian church — if you're old-time Bible Presbyterian — you probably grew up with an inconsistent dispensational position there as well.

The historic premillennial position includes a future Messianic kingdom view — there would be a different phase. Christ is ruling right now. We are his kingdom, but there would be a different rule of Christ where he's going to rule in person physically on this earth for 1,000 years. There'll be a glorious future kingdom on earth, for 1,000 years, which kingdom will continue in future ages. So it's not like Christ rules for 1,000 years and he's done. He continues really. The physicality of the kingdom continues for eternity from that point and it will be both political and spiritual. It's difficult to think of Christ as a king without the political aspects of that. The king rules, right? The king is the chief politician there and he is going to be ruling by force over some people because there would be unconverted people who end that millennium and then judgment comes.

The historical premillennialism teaches that there would be a future time of severe persecution to precede the return of Christ and described in Revelation 6 to 19 and perhaps also described in the synoptic Gospels, Matthew, Mark and Luke, and that the church will go through that period. There will be no such thing as what's been called a rapture where people the church would be taken and disappear into heaven. The only book you find that's not the Bible, it is the left behind series and that's 100% fiction. Probably more fictitious than Harry Potter — the left behind series.

The church will go through this period of difficulties of tribulation. So the post-millennial, the amillennial and the historic pre-millennial position all agree that the church will go or has gone through this period of great persecution. When the New Testament speaks of this period of persecution, they equate to one period in the history — the only comparison Jesus gives to the last things is to the flood as it was in the time of Noah. And the church at the time goes through that period of persecution of trials of tribulations as Noah was in the ark but would there be great grief being shut up in the ark for 14 months with all the animals in the world in the middle of a big storm.

The historical premillennial position also believes in two future resurrections. The resurrection of the righteous at the return of Christ, and then the resurrection of the unrighteous after the thousand years are completed. You see that in Revelation chapter 20 verses four through six. And then where we get the name — the second coming before the millennium, therefore pre-millennial position — the physical kingdom will follow the physical personal return of Christ in the earth for the promises to be fulfilled.

The tribulation timing connects to the identity of the church. As I mentioned, any of these positions that I've outlined require a post-tribulational return of Christ. All the reform positions have to do with the identity of the church. What is the church? Is that a new entity that became a Pentecost? Or is it something that began with Abraham in the Old Testament? These positions assume the latter, that the church is something that began with Abraham. The only way you can have a pre-tribulation return — Christ coming before the tribulation — is if you see the church as a completely different entity from Israel, and that period of tribulation is only to affect ethnic Israel and not the church. We're going to try to look at that later as well.

All right, so I'm in a good place to stop here as far as my notes go. We'll pick up Lord William next week with the actual arguments used for premillennialism, but that we're just going to briefly look at them and then in future times we'll come back to actually defend each one of these things. And then we also want to take a look at the tribulation as a future event, I'm going to try to show you why I believe that's the case. We do want to talk about the idea. I think it is a big disservice to the church to teach that somehow the church is going to be spared from future tribulation. I also want to spend some time trying to show why I believe the Bible teaches these two different resurrections of the righteous and the unrighteous. And then ultimately the last blessing would be whenever we get to it, showing the nature of the church that requires certain beliefs regarding the coming of Christ because of what the church is according to the scriptures.

All right? Any last question before we close? All right. So let's pray. Father, thank you that you're God who speaks and thank you that we can rely on your wisdom through the church to understand what your word says. We pray that you continue to guide us as we study these things. We pray that we will be accurate in all that we say. We pray in Jesus' name, Amen.
